featuredSetups.label: Crowded beaker
featuredSetups.description: Open a concentrated mixture with density cues visible.

featuredSetups.label: Dilute without losing solute
featuredSetups.description: Open the same solute amount in more liquid and compare the new concentration.

pageIntro.definition: Use one beaker to separate how concentration changes when you add solvent from how it changes when you add more solute.
pageIntro.keyTakeaway: Concentration is about how much solute is present in how much liquid.

v2.intuition: Use one beaker to separate what changes when you add solvent from what changes when you add more solute.
v2.whyItMatters: This is the foundational concentration story behind dilution, solution prep, and later equilibrium ideas, so the numerator-versus-denominator trade needs to be visible.

guidedSteps.title: Read one crowded beaker
guidedSteps.goal: Use a single beaker to read concentration as amount of solute per amount of liquid.
guidedSteps.doThis: Start with the crowded-beaker preset, keep the amount-versus-volume guide visible, and raise Solute amount once.
guidedSteps.notice: The particle picture gets denser and the concentration rises when more solute is packed into the same liquid volume.
guidedSteps.explain: Concentration compares how much solute is present with how much solution or solvent volume is available.

guidedSteps.title: Dilute without removing solute
guidedSteps.goal: See why adding solvent lowers concentration even when the solute amount stays the same.
guidedSteps.doThis: Apply the diluted-same-solute preset, then add more solvent without changing the solute amount.
guidedSteps.notice: The beaker looks less crowded and the concentration graph drops even though the amount of solute is unchanged.
guidedSteps.explain: Dilution changes the denominator. The same solute is spread through more liquid, so the concentration falls.

guidedSteps.title: Raise solute at fixed volume
guidedSteps.goal: Separate adding solute from adding solvent so the two concentration changes do not blur together.
guidedSteps.doThis: Switch to the more-solute-same-volume preset and increase Solute amount while the liquid volume stays fixed.
guidedSteps.notice: Now the concentration rises because the numerator is growing instead of the denominator.
guidedSteps.explain: You only understand a concentration change when you can name which quantity changed: more solute, more liquid, or both.

learned: Concentration compares how much solute is present with how much liquid is available.
Adding solvent can lower concentration without removing any solute.
Adding solute at fixed volume raises concentration for a different reason than dilution lowers it.
wrapUp.misconception: Dilution means the solute disappears or leaves the beaker.

nextConcepts.title: Solubility and Saturation
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: See when concentration hits a capacity limit

nextConcepts.title: Acid-Base / pH Intuition
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: Carry concentration ideas into acid strength and pH

wrapUp.freePlayPrompt: Build two different routes to the same concentration: one by adding solute and one by changing solvent volume.

paragraphs: Concentration becomes easier to trust when the amount of solute and the amount of liquid stay visible together. This bench keeps one vessel, a concentration readout, and a particle view in the same frame so crowding and dilution do not turn into slogans.
The key distinction is that adding solvent changes concentration without adding solute, while adding solute changes concentration in a different way.

keyIdeas: Concentration is about how much solute is present in how much liquid.
Dilution lowers concentration by increasing the amount of liquid while keeping the solute amount the same.
Adding solute and adding solvent can both change concentration, but for different reasons.

commonMisconception.myth: If the beaker looks fuller, that must mean there is more solute in it.
correction: A fuller beaker can simply mean there is more solvent present.
Dilution changes concentration even when the solute amount does not change.

workedExamples.title: Live worked example
workedExamples.intro: Use the current beaker state rather than a detached ratio problem.

items.title: Read the current concentration
items.prompt: For the current mixture, how concentrated is the beaker?

steps.label: 1. Read how much solute is present
steps.template: The beaker currently holds {{soluteAmountValue}} units of solute.

steps.label: 2. Read how much liquid is present
steps.template: The same solute is spread through {{solventVolumeValue}} units of solvent.

steps.label: 3. Read the concentration and density cue together
steps.template: That gives a concentration of about {{concentrationValue}}, with roughly {{particleCountValue}} visible particles in the beaker.

applyAction.label: Apply the diluted case
applyAction.observationHint: The solute amount stays fixed while the particle view spreads out in the larger volume.

miniChallenge.prompt: Make the beaker clearly less concentrated without removing any solute.
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether to change the amount of solute, the amount of solvent, or both before you try it.
miniChallenge.answer: You need to add solvent while keeping the solute amount fixed.
miniChallenge.explanation: That is the cleanest way to model dilution rather than simply making a different mixture.

equations.label: Concentration rule
equations.meaning: Keeps the beaker honest by relating amount of solute to amount of liquid.

equations.label: Dilution story
equations.meaning: Shows that dilution keeps the solute amount fixed while the liquid amount changes.

variableLinks.label: Solute amount
variableLinks.description: Changes how much dissolved material is present in the beaker.

variableLinks.label: Solvent volume
variableLinks.description: Changes how much liquid the same solute is spread through.

presets.label: Crowded beaker
presets.description: A concentrated mixture in a smaller liquid volume.

presets.label: Diluted same solute
presets.description: The same solute amount spread through more liquid.

presets.label: More solute, same volume
presets.description: A concentration change caused by adding more solute.

overlays.label: Density cue
overlays.shortDescription: Show how crowded or sparse the particle view is.
whatToNotice: The same number of particles can look more dilute when they occupy more volume.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes concentration visible rather than purely numerical.

overlays.label: Amount-volume guide
overlays.shortDescription: Keep amount and volume cues visible together.
whatToNotice: Changing liquid volume and changing solute amount are not the same operation.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps dilution distinct from simply adding more dissolved material.

overlays.label: Particle motion
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the particle drift visible inside the beaker.
whatToNotice: The particles are still moving even when the concentration readout is steady.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the beaker feeling like a live mixture rather than a static picture.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the beaker and the concentration graph in view together.

items.text: Adding solvent lowers concentration even when the solute amount has not changed.
Adding solute raises concentration for a different reason: there are simply more dissolved particles in the same amount of liquid.

predictionMode.title: Predict
predictionMode.intro: Predict how the beaker will change before you apply the new setup.

items.prompt: If solvent volume increases while solute amount stays fixed, what should happen first?

choices.label: The concentration should drop because the same solute is spread through more liquid.
The concentration should rise because the beaker is fuller.

items.explanation: Dilution lowers concentration without changing solute amount.
items.observationHint: Compare the density cue before and after the new solvent volume is applied.

quickTest.title: Quick test
quickTest.intro: Answer from the live beaker story, not from a memorized slogan.

questions.prompt: What is the cleanest description of dilution on this bench?

choices.label: Lowering concentration by adding more liquid while keeping solute amount fixed.
Lowering concentration by removing solute first.
Any change that makes the beaker look fuller.

questions.explanation: Dilution is specifically about changing the amount of liquid around the same solute.

questions.prompt: Which change raises concentration by a different mechanism from dilution?

choices.label: Adding more solute while keeping the same liquid volume.
Adding more solvent while keeping solute fixed.

questions.explanation: Adding solute changes how much dissolved material is present rather than how much liquid it is spread through.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use dilution honestly: do not smuggle in a different solute amount.

items.title: Dilute without losing solute
items.prompt: Starting from a fairly crowded beaker, lower the concentration clearly while keeping the solute amount near the same starting value.
items.successMessage: You made the mixture more dilute without changing it into a different solute case. The concentration dropped because the same dissolved material occupied more liquid.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a beaker with dissolved particles, a concentration readout, and controls for solute amount and solvent volume.
One graph shows how concentration changes with solvent volume, and a second shows how concentration changes with solute amount.

seo.title: Concentration and Dilution
seo.description: Explore concentration, dilution, and the difference between adding solute and adding solvent on a live chemistry beaker.
